message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 24
Recent Green phase: added 50% reimbursement for high enchantment. New constants HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD=8, HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE=0.5. New helper reimbursementRateFor(item). payoutForDamage now takes (damage, items[]) and finds the matching item.

Working directory: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_17-48-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking-2

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Tests are run with `pnpm test`.

Upcoming tests:
- Dragon material → 100% reimbursement (essentially "ignore" the high-enchantment halving when material is dragon)
- Both apply → 50% wins (dragon-material + enchantment 9 → 50%)
- The "dragon wins over standard" but "50% wins over dragon" makes a 3-rule precedence: 50% > dragon > standard.

But spec actually says: "Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50% of the damage amount. Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed." Then in examples: "dragon-material sword, enchantment 9 → 50% wins". So 50% applies if either trigger fires... no actually high-ench applies (50%), dragon also applies (100%), the SMALLER applies.

The minimum-rate wins. So instead of a fixed rate hierarchy, it's: take MIN of applicable rates.

Don't anticipate — current state has only 1 rule (high-ench halving). Refactor for current state only.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
ESLint reports zero violations. Tests are green at 24/24. Let me now compute APP mass and McCabe for each function.

## PRE Measurements

**ESLint smells**: 0 violations
**Cognitive complexity**: 0 reported (all functions below threshold)

### Function-by-function APP + McCabe

**`sumBy`** (line 50-51): 
- Constants: 0, Bindings: 4 (items, f, acc, x), Invocations: 2 (reduce, f), Conditionals: 0, Loops: 1 (reduce), Assignments: 0
- Mass: 0+4+4+0+5+0 = 13, McCabe: 1

**`baseFor`**: bindings 1, invocation lookup ~0. Mass ≈ 1, McCabe: 1

**`surchargeFor`**: Mass ~15, McCabe: 2 (ternary inside sumBy)

**`totalSurchargeFor`**: Mass ~6, McCabe: 1

**`totalDiscountFor`**: Mass ~14, McCabe: 2

**`premiumFor`**: Mass ~22, McCabe: 1

**`groupsByType`**: Mass ~18, McCabe: 1

**`formsCompleteBlock`**: 
- Bindings: 2 (group), Invocations: 2 (length, has), Conditionals: 0, Loops: 0, &&: +1 branch
- Mass ~9, McCabe: 2

**`baseForGroup`**: 
- ternary `?:` → +1
- Mass ~12, McCabe: 2

**`totalBaseFor`**: Mass ~6, McCabe: 1

**`insuranceSumFor`**: Mass ~10, McCabe: 1

**`newPolicy`**: Mass ~10, McCabe: 1

**`reimbursementRateFor`** (line 110-113):
- Constants: 2 (0, 1), Bindings: 1 (item), Invocations: 0, Conditionals: 0, ternary +1, `?.` +1, `??`+1 (chained branch). Loops: 0, Assignments: 0
- Mass: 2 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 3 (plus ternary 4, loops 0) ≈ 7
- McCabe: 1 + 1 (ternary) + 1 (optional chaining) + 1 (??) = ~4 (using lenient interpretation, or 2 if counting only ternary). Let's say McCabe: 3

**`payoutForDamage`** (line 115-119):
- Constants: 1 (0), Bindings: 4 (damage, items, item, reimbursed), Invocations: 4 (find, lambda, reimbursementRateFor, Math.max), Conditionals: 0, Loops: 0, Assignments: 0
- Mass: 1 + 4 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 13
- McCabe: 1

**`processClaim`** (line 121-126):
- Constants: 0, Bindings: 4 (policy, incident, grossPayout, payout), Invocations: 4 (sumBy, lambda, Math.min, payoutForDamage), Conditionals: 0, Loops: 0, Assignments: 1 (-=)
- Mass: 0 + 4 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 6 = 18
- McCabe: 1

**`quoteFor`**: Mass ~14, McCabe: 1

**`handleQuote`** (line 134-139): 
- Constants: 0, Bindings: 4, Invocations: 4 (push, newPolicy, quoteFor), Conditionals: 0, Loops: 0, Assignments: 2 (+=1, push counts)
- Mass: ~18, McCabe: 1

**`handleStep`** (line 141-144):
- Ternary +1
- Mass ~12, McCabe: 2

**`processScenario`**: 
- Mass ~15, McCabe: 1

### Worst Offenders

The highest McCabe scores are tied around 2-3. The function I want to look at closely is `reimbursementRateFor` — it was just added with multiple decision points (ternary + `?.` + `??`). But it's small and clear.

Actually, looking more broadly, the most interesting refactoring opportunity is the **structural inconsistency**: we have `SURCHARGE_RULES` and `DISCOUNT_RULES` as data-driven tables, but the new `reimbursementRateFor` uses an ad-hoc ternary. We can introduce a parallel pattern.

However, the user's hint warns: **"current state has only 1 rule (high-ench halving). Refactor for current state only. Don't anticipate."**

So introducing a `REIMBURSEMENT_RULES` table for one rule would be premature abstraction (violates Rule 4: Fewest Elements).

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `reimbursementRateFor(item)`: Reveals intent — "what rate to reimburse this item at". Clear.
- `payoutForDamage(damage, items[])`: Takes the full items list to find a match. The name says "for damage" but it also needs items context. Could be `payoutFor(damage, items)` or kept. The current name is fine.
- `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`: Wordy but clear and distinguish from surcharge constants. Keep.

One thing that stands out: in `payoutForDamage`, we do `items.find(...)` to look up the item. If the item isn't found, `reimbursementRateFor(undefined)` returns 1 (full reimbursement). This is implicit fallback. Let me check if there's a smell here.

Looking at the function:
```typescript
const payoutForDamage = (damage: Damage, items: Item[]): number => {
  const item = items.find((i) => i.type === damage.itemType);
  const reimbursed = damage.amount * reimbursementRateFor(item);
  return Math.max(0, reimbursed - DEDUCTIBLE);
};
```

The intermediate `reimbursed` is named after its meaning. That's good Rule 2.

## Looking for the strongest refactoring candidate

The most subtle issue: `reimbursementRateFor` takes `Item | undefined`. This is an awkward signature. Let me think — the alternative would be to do the find inside, but then it needs `items` too. Or restructure so the caller always passes an item.

Actually, a cleaner shape: have `payoutForDamage` find the item, and inline the rate logic OR pass the found item to a function that only takes `Item`. Let's count both options.

**Option A**: Keep as-is. (Current)

**Option B**: Make `reimbursementRateFor` require `Item` (not `Item | undefined`), and handle the missing case at the caller.

```typescript
const reimbursementRateFor = (item: Item): number =>
  (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD
    ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : 1;

const payoutForDamage = (damage: Damage, items: Item[]): number => {
  const item = items.find((i) => i.type === damage.itemType);
  const rate = item ? reimbursementRateFor(item) : 1;
  const reimbursed = damage.amount * rate;
  return Math.max(0, reimbursed - DEDUCTIBLE);
};
```

Adding a branch at caller, removing `?.` from helper. Net McCabe: same. Worse.

**Option C**: Inline `reimbursementRateFor` since it's only called once.

This violates Rule 2 a bit (named concept "reimbursement rate" disappears) but reduces total functions. Trade-off uncertain.

**Option D**: Use a lookup approach: compute rate as a property, then payout.

Actually, the cleanest insight is: the current code is already clean for a single rule. Let me look at this from a different angle.

Let me actually try one concrete refactor and measure: simplify the `payoutForDamage` to be less intermediate-heavy.

Wait — let me look at this differently. Is there duplication or unclear intent? Let me look at the relationship between `surchargeFor` (rule table) and `reimbursementRateFor` (single rule).

Actually, looking again at `reimbursementRateFor`:
```typescript
const reimbursementRateFor = (item: Item | undefined): number =>
  (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD
    ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : 1;
```

This expresses: "if highly enchanted, halve. Otherwise full." Clear and minimal. McCabe 2-3 depending on interpretation. APP mass ~7.

Let me look at the broader question: is there anywhere that "feels off"?

Looking at `surchargeFor`:
```typescript
const surchargeFor = (item: Item): number => {
  const base = baseFor(item);
  return sumBy(SURCHARGE_RULES, (rule) => (rule.applies(item) ? base * rule.rate : 0));
};
```

The pattern `(applies ? value : 0)` summed across rules is clever but could be clearer with a `filter`:

```typescript
const surchargeFor = (item: Item): number => {
  const base = baseFor(item);
  return sumBy(
    SURCHARGE_RULES.filter((rule) => rule.applies(item)),
    (rule) => base * rule.rate,
  );
};
```

Let me count: 
- Before: bindings (item, base, rule) = 3, invocations (baseFor, sumBy, applies) = 3, ternary +1 conditional
- After: bindings 3, invocations (baseFor, sumBy, filter, applies) = 4, conditional 0
- Mass before: 3+6+4+0+5+0 = 18. McCabe: 2 (ternary).
- Mass after: 3+8+0+0+10 (two loops) = 21. McCabe: 1.

Worse mass, better McCabe. Mixed result — not a clear win.

Let me think about this kata differently. The hint warns me to focus on the current state, not anticipate. Given that:
- ESLint: clean
- All cognitive complexities: well below threshold
- All McCabe: 1-3
- APP masses: reasonable (mostly under 20)
- Naming: clear after recent rename

Let me look at one more potential simplification: there's the optional chaining in `reimbursementRateFor`. Since `find` can return undefined, but actually in practice — can a damage refer to an item not in items? Per spec semantics, damages are always for items in the policy. Let me check the test data quickly.

All current tests have damages whose itemType is in the policy. The `| undefined` handling in `reimbursementRateFor` is defensive but unused. However, removing it would require a non-null assertion at the call site, which is worse.

Let me try a different concrete refactoring: **reduce the McCabe of `reimbursementRateFor` by removing the unused defensive `| undefined` path**, simplifying to `Item`:

content.text:
Tests still 24/24 green. ESLint still clean. Let me compute POST measurements for the two changed functions.

## POST Measurements

**`reimbursementRateFor`** (now `(item: Item) => number`):
- Constants: 2 (0, 1), Bindings: 1 (item), Invocations: 0, Conditionals: 0, ternary +1 (counts as conditional), `??` +1 branch
- Mass: 2 + 1 + 0 + 4 (ternary) + 0 + 0 = 7
- McCabe: 1 + 1 (ternary) + 1 (`??`) = 3 → wait, previously had 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 (`?.`) = 4. So McCabe dropped by 1.

**`payoutForDamage`**:
- Constants: 1 (0), Bindings: 4 (damage, items, item, reimbursed), Invocations: 4 (find, lambda, reimbursementRateFor, Math.max), Conditionals: 1 (if), Loops: 0, Assignments: 0
- Mass: 1 + 4 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 17 (was 13, +4 from added guard)
- McCabe: 1 + 1 (if) = 2 (was 1, +1 from guard)

### Delta Analysis

| Function | Metric | PRE | POST | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| reimbursementRateFor | APP mass | 7 | 7 | 0 |
| reimbursementRateFor | McCabe | 4 | 3 | **-1** |
| payoutForDamage | APP mass | 13 | 17 | +4 |
| payoutForDamage | McCabe | 1 | 2 | +1 |
| **Combined** | **APP** | **20** | **24** | **+4** |
| **Combined** | **McCabe** | **5** | **5** | **0** |

This refactor traded complexity between two functions but the net is **mass went UP by 4 and combined McCabe is flat**. That's a worse position.

The Rule 2 (intent) argument: `reimbursementRateFor` now has a more honest signature (no defensive optional) — that's a clarity win. But the cost is added structural mass.

Per the discipline: **if a measurement got worse, revert and try a different angle.**

content.text:
Tests still green after revert. Let me look for a different angle.

Looking at the structure of `payoutForDamage` and `reimbursementRateFor` together — there's a subtle thing: `reimbursementRateFor` accepts `undefined` to handle the "item not found" case by returning 1. But the variable name `reimbursementRateFor` doesn't reveal "and 1 if no item found". The function signature carries information that the name doesn't.

Let me try another angle: **merge `reimbursementRateFor` into a method that operates on damage+items together**, eliminating the awkward optional-item shape:

```typescript
const reimbursementRateForDamage = (damage: Damage, items: Item[]): number => {
  const item = items.find((i) => i.type === damage.itemType);
  return (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD
    ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : 1;
};

const payoutForDamage = (damage: Damage, items: Item[]): number =>
  Math.max(0, damage.amount * reimbursementRateForDamage(damage, items) - DEDUCTIBLE);
```

Let me count this:

**`reimbursementRateForDamage`** (new): 
- Constants: 2 (0, 1), Bindings: 3 (damage, items, item), Invocations: 2 (find, lambda), Conditionals: 0, ternary +1, `?.` +1, `??` +1
- Mass: 2 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 13
- McCabe: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4

**`payoutForDamage`** (new):
- Bindings: 2 (damage, items), Invocations: 3 (reimbursementRateForDamage, Math.max), Constants: 1 (0), Conditionals: 0, Loops: 0, Assignments: 0
- Mass: 1 + 2 + 6 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 9
- McCabe: 1

Combined POST: Mass 13+9 = 22, McCabe 4+1 = 5
Current PRE: Mass 7+13 = 20, McCabe 4+1 = 5

Combined mass goes UP by 2. McCabe same. Worse trade.

Let me try the inverse: **inline `reimbursementRateFor` since it's a single-use, single-condition rate calc**. The named concept `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD` already carry the meaning.

```typescript
const payoutForDamage = (damage: Damage, items: Item[]): number => {
  const item = items.find((i) => i.type === damage.itemType);
  const rate = (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_THRESHOLD
    ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : 1;
  return Math.max(0, damage.amount * rate - DEDUCTIBLE);
};
```

Counting:
- Constants: 3 (0, 1, and 0 again), Bindings: 4 (damage, items, item, rate), Invocations: 3 (find, lambda, Math.max), Conditionals: 0, ternary +1, `?.` +1, `??` +1, Loops: 0, Assignments: 0
- Mass: 3 + 4 + 6 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 17
- McCabe: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4

Combined POST: 17 (one function), McCabe 4
Combined PRE: 20 (two functions), McCabe 5
**Mass: 20 → 17 (-3), McCabe: 5 → 4 (-1), functions: 2 → 1 (Rule 4 win)**

But: loses the named concept "reimbursement rate". That's a Rule 2 cost.

Hmm. Rule 2 trumps APP. The name `reimbursementRateFor` does carry domain meaning. Inlining might hurt clarity.

But the hint warned: "current state has only 1 rule (high-ench halving). Refactor for current state only." If only one rule exists, having a dedicated function is arguably premature. The named constants already encode the concept.

Let me apply this and measure:

content.text:
Tests still 24/24 green, ESLint still clean. Reminder still not applicable — single focused refactor task.

But wait — I need to reconsider this. The hint specifically said: "Dragon material → 100% reimbursement" and "Both apply → 50% wins" and "the SMALLER applies". This is forthcoming, but the hint also said "Don't anticipate — refactor for current state only."

Inlining now means when the next test adds dragon-material, the green-phase agent will need to extract again. That's fine — TDD encourages emergent design.

However, I'm reconsidering Rule 2 here. The named function `reimbursementRateFor(item)` was a clear concept extracted just one cycle ago. Inlining it back undoes that work. The function expressed "this is how we determine reimbursement rate", which IS valuable abstraction even with one rule — it's the seam that the next test will extend.

Let me re-examine: is inlining actually a Rule 2 loss? The inlined version still has `rate` as a named local. The trade is:
- Lose: function name `reimbursementRateFor` (a named, reusable abstraction)
- Gain: -3 mass, -1 McCabe, -1 function

Given that Rule 2 (intent) trumps APP mass for this priority order, and the named function was clearer, I should revert this too. The discipline says: **clarity over numbers**.

Let me revert:

content.text:
Final state: 24/24 tests pass, ESLint clean. File is back to its post-Green state.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring Attempts**:

**Attempt 1**: Tightened `reimbursementRateFor` signature to `Item` (non-optional) with guard clause `if (!item) return 0;` in `payoutForDamage`.
- PRE/POST for `reimbursementRateFor`: APP 7 → 7 (Δ 0), McCabe 4 → 3 (Δ -1)
- PRE/POST for `payoutForDamage`: APP 13 → 17 (Δ +4), McCabe 1 → 2 (Δ +1)
- **Combined**: APP 20 → 24 (Δ +4 worse), McCabe 5 → 5 (Δ 0 flat)
- **Reverted** — net mass got worse, McCabe just shifted between functions.

**Attempt 2**: Inlined `reimbursementRateFor` into `payoutForDamage` (single-use helper).
- PRE/POST: 2 functions (mass 20, McCabe 5) → 1 function (mass 17, McCabe 4)
- Pure metric win: APP -3, McCabe -1, function count -1
- **Reverted** — Rule 2 (intent) trumps APP per priority order. The named function `reimbursementRateFor` carries a domain concept (the "rate at which an item is reimbursed") that was just extracted one Green cycle ago. The intent expressed by the name is more valuable than 3 units of mass.

**Final State Measurements** (no change from PRE):

| Function | APP mass | McCabe |
|---|---|---|
| reimbursementRateFor | 7 | 4 |
| payoutForDamage | 13 | 1 |
| processClaim | 18 | 1 |
| surchargeFor | 15 | 2 |
| baseForGroup | 12 | 2 |
| handleStep | 12 | 2 |
| formsCompleteBlock | 9 | 2 |
| (others) | <15 | 1 |

- ESLint smells: 0 → 0
- Cognitive complexity violations: 0 → 0
- All functions: McCabe ≤ 4
- Naming: `reimbursementRateFor` reveals intent clearly for current single-rule state

**Refactoring**: None applied — two concrete attempts measured and reverted because both either worsened net APP mass (attempt 1) or sacrificed named intent that Rule 2 protects above mass reduction (attempt 2).

**Tests**: 24/24 passing

**File**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_17-48-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts`
